schools.summary: The call of Abram: 'now the LORD had said unto Abram, Get thee out of thy country, and from thy kindred, and from thy father's house, unto a land that I will shew thee' — God calling Abram to leave his country (the call given first in Ur, Acts 7:2-4, and renewed at Haran, the pluperfect 'had said' connecting the call to Abram's departure from Ur, 11:31, the divine summons to forsake all and follow God to an unknown land); the threefold leaving — 'thy country... thy kindred... thy father's house' (Abram forsaking his native land, his wider kin, and his own family, the call demanding a complete separation from all that was dear and familiar, the test of Abram's faith and obedience, to leave all at God's word); 'unto a land that I will shew thee' (an unnamed, unknown land, Abram going out not knowing whither he went, Hebrews 11:8, the call requiring faith, to leave the known for the unknown on the bare word of God, the obedience of faith, trusting God's promise and guidance, the pattern of the believer's pilgrimage) — Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott, Henry, Calvin, Clarke.

themes: Now the LORD had said unto Abram, Get thee out of thy country, and from thy kindred, and from thy father's house, unto a land that I will shew thee
the call of Abram to leave his country for the land God would show him
the threefold renunciation - country, kindred, father's house
the obedience of faith, leaving the known for the unknown (Hebrews 11:8)

schools.summary: The promise to Abram: 'and I will make of thee a great nation, and I will bless thee, and make thy name great; and thou shalt be a blessing' — God promising Abram a numerous posterity, a great nation (Israel, and the multitude of his spiritual seed, the promise of a great nation to a man whose wife was barren, 11:30, the human impossibility against which God's power would be displayed); God blessing Abram 'and make thy name great' (God's favor and blessing on Abram, and the exalting of his name, in striking contrast to the builders of Babel, who sought to 'make us a name,' 11:4, by their own pride — God himself making Abram's name great, Abram's name, honored by God, revered through all generations); 'thou shalt be a blessing' (Abram not only blessed but a blessing, a fountain of blessing to others, through him and his seed blessing flowing to the nations, v.3, the called man made a means of grace to the world, the blessing of Abram to overflow to all) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And I will make of thee a great nation, and I will bless thee, and make thy name great; and thou shalt be a blessing
the promise of a great nation, blessing, and a great name
God making Abram's name great, against Babel's self-made name (11:4)
Abram made a fountain of blessing to the nations

schools.summary: Calvin and the dominant evangelical reading (the Gospel promise): 'and I will bless them that bless thee, and curse him that curseth thee: and in thee shall all families of the earth be blessed' — God so identifying himself with Abram and his seed that he would bless those who blessed him and curse those who cursed him (God's protecting favor over Abram and his line, the friends of Abram's seed God's friends, the enemies God's enemies, the special providence over the chosen line); 'in thee shall all families of the earth be blessed' as the Gospel promise of blessing to all nations in Abram's seed, Christ — all the families/nations of the earth blessed in Abram, that is, in his seed, the Messiah, who would spring from him (Galatians 3:8, 'the scripture, foreseeing that God would justify the heathen through faith, preached before the gospel unto Abraham'), the promise to Abram's seed, 'which is Christ' (Galatians 3:16), in Christ, of Abram's line, the Gospel preached to all nations, and all who believe, of every nation, blessed with justification and salvation, the universal blessing of the Gospel, the heart of the Abrahamic covenant; a secondary aspect noting Abram and his posterity (the true religion, the Scriptures, the line of the church) a channel of blessing to the world — Calvin, Gill, Clarke.

debates.question: How are 'all families of the earth' blessed in Abram?

sides.position: In Abram's seed, the Messiah, Christ — the Gospel promise of justification and salvation to all who believe (the dominant evangelical reading, Galatians 3:8, 16)
More generally, through Abram and his posterity — the channel of the true religion and the line of the church to the world

themes: And I will bless them that bless thee, and curse him that curseth thee: and in thee shall all families of the earth be blessed
the promise of blessing to all families of the earth in Abram (the crux of the Gospel promise)
all nations blessed in Abram's seed, Christ (Galatians 3:8, 16) vs a general channel of blessing
the Gospel preached to Abraham, the heart of the Abrahamic covenant

schools.summary: Abram's departure: 'so Abram departed, as the LORD had spoken unto him; and Lot went with him: and Abram was seventy and five years old when he departed out of Haran' — Abram obeying the call, departing from Haran as God had commanded (the prompt, unquestioning obedience of faith, Hebrews 11:8, he going out, trusting God's word, to the land God would show him, the obedience that followed the call, vv.1-3); 'Lot went with him' (Abram's nephew Lot accompanying him, the family of the righteous traveling together, Lot, who would share in Abram's journeyings and later separate, ch. 13, the companion of Abram's pilgrimage); Abram seventy-five when he left Haran (an advanced age to begin a pilgrimage to an unknown land, the obedience of faith at a time of life when men seek rest, Abram, old and childless, setting out on God's promise, the dating of the departure marking the beginning of the patriarchal history) — Ellicott, Gill, Henry, Clarke.

themes: So Abram departed, as the LORD had spoken unto him; and Lot went with him: and Abram was seventy and five years old when he departed out of Haran
Abram's obedient departure from Haran
the prompt obedience of faith (Hebrews 11:8)
the old and childless man setting out on God's promise

schools.summary: Abram's journey into Canaan: 'and Abram took Sarai his wife, and Lot his brother's son, and all their substance that they had gathered, and the souls that they had gotten in Haran; and they went forth to go into the land of Canaan; and into the land of Canaan they came' — Abram setting out with his wife Sarai and his nephew Lot (the household of the patriarch, the company of the pilgrimage to Canaan); they taking 'all their substance (their possessions, flocks, goods) and the souls that they had gotten in Haran' (their accumulated wealth, and the 'souls,' the servants, dependents, and perhaps proselytes, those gained or won, some say converted to the true religion, in Haran, the considerable household of Abram, with its possessions and persons, some taking 'the souls they had gotten' as those won to the faith); 'into the land of Canaan they came' (Abram reaching the land God had promised to show him, v.1, the pilgrimage's first goal attained, Canaan, the promised land, entered, the patriarch arriving in the land of promise, to sojourn there as a stranger, Hebrews 11:9) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And Abram took Sarai his wife, and Lot his brother's son, and all their substance... and the souls that they had gotten in Haran... and into the land of Canaan they came
Abram's journey with his household into Canaan
the possessions and dependents (some, proselytes) of the patriarch
reaching the promised land, to sojourn as a stranger (Hebrews 11:9)

schools.summary: Abram at Shechem and the Canaanite in the land: 'and Abram passed through the land unto the place of Sichem, unto the plain of Moreh. And the Canaanite was then in the land' — Abram traveling through Canaan to Shechem, a central place in the land (the oak/plain of Moreh, a notable spot, the patriarch journeying through the land that was promised to his seed); on 'the Canaanite was then in the land,' Clarke and some holding the phrase a later note or interpolation (seeming to be written from the standpoint of a time when the Canaanites were no longer in the land, after their expulsion by Israel, the note a later editorial gloss, or evidence of post-Mosaic composition, explaining that, in Abram's day, the land was still held by the Canaanites); Ellicott and the conservative reading holding it no sign of post-Mosaic authorship nor an interpolation (the note simply stating that, when Abram came, the Canaanites already occupied the land, so that Abram was a stranger in a land not yet his, Moses, the author, writing it as a plain historical observation, the Canaanites being still in the land in his own day, before the conquest, the phrase requiring no later hand); whatever its bearing on authorship, the note marking that Abram, to whom the land was promised, found it already inhabited by the Canaanites, a stranger and sojourner in the land of promise (Hebrews 11:9), possessing none of it, walking by faith in God's promise to give it to his seed (v.7) — Ellicott, Gill, Clarke, Calvin.

debates.question: Does 'the Canaanite was then in the land' indicate post-Mosaic authorship?

sides.position: A later editorial gloss / evidence of post-Mosaic composition — written when the Canaanites were no longer in the land
No sign of post-Mosaic authorship — a plain note by Moses that the land was already occupied by the Canaanites in Abram's day

themes: And Abram passed through the land unto the place of Sichem, unto the plain of Moreh. And the Canaanite was then in the land
Abram's passing to Shechem and the note of the Canaanite (the crux of authorship)
a later gloss / post-Mosaic note vs a plain Mosaic observation
Abram a stranger in the land promised to his seed (Hebrews 11:9)

schools.summary: The LORD appears and promises the land: 'and the LORD appeared unto Abram, and said, Unto thy seed will I give this land: and there builded he an altar unto the LORD, who appeared unto him' — God appearing to Abram (the first recorded appearance of God to him in Canaan, the LORD confirming his presence and promise by a visible manifestation, the theophany that sealed the covenant promise, God drawing near to his servant in the promised land); 'Unto thy seed will I give this land' (God now naming the land, the very land of Canaan where Abram stood, and promising it to Abram's seed, the land-promise added to the seed-promise, though Abram himself would possess none of it, Acts 7:5, his seed, Israel, and ultimately the heavenly inheritance, inheriting the land, the covenant promise of the land confirmed); 'there builded he an altar unto the LORD, who appeared unto him' (Abram building an altar to worship the God who appeared to him, the first of his altars in Canaan, the patriarch's worship, marking the place of God's appearing, Abram, a stranger in the land, setting up the worship of the true God, the altar a witness of his faith and devotion) — Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott, Calvin, Gill, Henry.

themes: And the LORD appeared unto Abram, and said, Unto thy seed will I give this land: and there builded he an altar unto the LORD
the appearing of the LORD and the promise of the land to Abram's seed
the land promised to Abram's seed, though he himself possessed none (Acts 7:5)
Abram's altar and worship of the God who appeared

schools.summary: Abram's altar at Bethel: 'and he removed from thence unto a mountain on the east of Bethel, and pitched his tent, having Bethel on the west, and Hai on the east: and there he builded an altar unto the LORD, and called upon the name of the LORD' — Abram journeying on and pitching his tent between Bethel and Ai (the tent-dwelling of the pilgrim patriarch, Hebrews 11:9, dwelling in tents, the place named, where Abram would worship); 'there he builded an altar unto the LORD' (Abram building another altar, his second in Canaan, wherever he went setting up the worship of God, the patriarch's habit of worship, the altar marking his devotion in each place of his sojourn); 'called upon the name of the LORD' (Abram publicly worshiping and praying, calling on the name of the LORD, invoking and proclaiming God, the public profession of the true religion amid the Canaanites, the patriarch's prayer and worship, a witness to the surrounding heathen, Abram, a stranger in the land, keeping and proclaiming the worship of the true God) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Henry, Calvin.

themes: And he removed from thence unto a mountain on the east of Bethel, and pitched his tent... and there he builded an altar unto the LORD, and called upon the name of the LORD
Abram's altar at Bethel and his calling on the name of the LORD
the pilgrim's tent-dwelling (Hebrews 11:9) and his habit of worship
the public profession of the true God amid the Canaanites

schools.summary: Abram journeys south: 'and Abram journeyed, going on still toward the south' — Abram continuing his journey, moving by stages toward the south (the Negev, the southern part of Canaan, the pilgrim patriarch traveling through the land, the gradual movement of Abram and his household through the country promised to his seed); Abram going 'still toward the south' (continuing his journey, not settling permanently, but moving as a pilgrim and stranger through the land, the tent-dwelling sojourner traversing Canaan, the patriarch's life of pilgrimage, Hebrews 11:9, dwelling in tents and journeying); Abram's journeying toward the south bringing him toward Egypt (where the famine would drive him, v.10, the movement of the patriarch under God's providence, the pilgrim life of Abram, journeying through the land of promise without a settled possession, Acts 7:5, the stranger and sojourner moving on) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin.

themes: And Abram journeyed, going on still toward the south
Abram's continued journey toward the south
the tent-dwelling pilgrim traversing the land of promise (Hebrews 11:9)
the journey bringing him toward Egypt under providence

schools.summary: The famine and the descent to Egypt: 'and there was a famine in the land: and Abram went down into Egypt to sojourn there; for the famine was grievous in the land' — a famine striking the land of Canaan, the very land of promise (the trial of Abram's faith, scarcely arrived in the promised land, he meeting famine, the testing of the patriarch, that the land flowing with promise should be visited with want, God's providence permitting the trial); 'Abram went down into Egypt to sojourn there' (driven by the famine, Abram going down to Egypt, well-watered by the Nile, often a refuge in famine, to sojourn for a time, some commending his prudence in seeking relief, others noting that he went without express direction from God, and that the descent into Egypt led him into temptation and sin, the deception over Sarai, vv.11-13, the trial that exposed Abram's weakness of faith); 'the famine was grievous in the land' (the famine severe, pressing Abram to seek relief, the grievousness of the want driving him to Egypt, the hard providence that tested the patriarch's faith and brought him into danger) — Ellicott, Gill, Henry, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And there was a famine in the land: and Abram went down into Egypt to sojourn there; for the famine was grievous in the land
the famine and Abram's going down into Egypt
the trial of Abram's faith by want in the very land of promise
the descent to Egypt leading into temptation

schools.summary: Abram's fear nearing Egypt: 'and it came to pass, when he was come near to enter into Egypt, that he said unto Sarai his wife, Behold now, I know that thou art a fair woman to look upon' — as Abram approached Egypt, he speaking to Sarai of his fear (the occasion of the deception, vv.12-13, the patriarch, anticipating danger in a strange and, he feared, lawless land, devising a plan); Abram saying 'I know that thou art a fair woman to look upon' (Sarai, though advanced in years, sixty-five or so, still beautiful, her beauty the occasion of Abram's fear, lest the Egyptians, taken with her, should kill him to take her, the cause of the danger Abram anticipated); Abram's fear, looking to the danger and devising a scheme (vv.12-13), showing the weakness of his faith at this point (he who left all on God's promise now fearing man and contriving a deception, the trial of Egypt exposing the patriarch's frailty, even the father of the faithful, in fear, faltering) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, JFB, Clarke.

themes: And it came to pass, when he was come near to enter into Egypt, that he said unto Sarai his wife, Behold now, I know that thou art a fair woman to look upon
Abram's fear as he neared Egypt, because of Sarai's beauty
the weakness of faith in the father of the faithful
Abram fearing man and devising a scheme

schools.summary: Abram's fear of being killed: 'therefore it shall come to pass, when the Egyptians shall see thee, that they shall say, This is his wife: and they will kill me, but they will save thee alive' — Abram fearing that the Egyptians, seeing Sarai's beauty and learning she was his wife, would kill him (the husband, the obstacle) and take her (his fear of the lawless violence he supposed in Egypt, the danger that prompted his deception, v.13); Abram anticipating being killed for his wife's sake (his fear of death driving him to the scheme of calling Sarai his sister, v.13, the weakness of faith that looked to the danger and trusted his own contrivance rather than God's protection, the patriarch, fearing man, seeking to save his life by a half-truth); Abram's reasoning, while not groundless (such things did happen in the ancient world), leading him into a course of deception that endangered Sarai's honor and dishonored his own faith (the fear of man bringing a snare, Proverbs 29:25, the trial exposing Abram's weakness, that even the father of the faithful, in a strait, leaned on his own wit) — Gill, Calvin.

themes: Therefore it shall come to pass, when the Egyptians shall see thee, that they shall say, This is his wife: and they will kill me, but they will save thee alive
Abram's fear that the Egyptians would kill him for Sarai
the dread of death driving him to deception
the fear of man a snare (Proverbs 29:25), exposing Abram's weakness

schools.summary: Gill and many (a sin/fault of unbelief): 'say, I pray thee, thou art my sister' — Abram, fearing for his life, putting into Sarai's mouth a half-truth meant to deceive (though Sarai was indeed his half-sister, his sister by his father, 20:12, the intent to conceal that she was his wife, and so to deceive, the deception springing from unbelief and the fear of man, even the strongest believer liable to sin, and Abram, famed for his faith, here falling into unbelief and into a fault that endangered his wife's honor); 'that it may be well with me for thy sake; and my soul shall live because of thee' (Abram's motive self-preservation, he seeking to save his own life by the deception, the fear of death, not faith in God's protection, governing the scheme, the motive marking the weakness of his faith, he trusting his own wit rather than God to preserve him); the half-truth, far from securing safety, bringing Sarai into Pharaoh's house and endangering the promise (vv.14-15), God having to intervene to protect Sarai and the covenant (v.17), the stratagem of unbelief bringing trouble, and God's faithfulness, not Abram's wit, preserving them — Gill, Clarke.

Ellicott and some (the mitigating truth): 'my sister' true literally, as Sarai was his half-sister (his sister by his father, 20:12), not a direct lie but an equivocation (a suppression of the whole truth), some judging Abram's act prudent self-preservation, others a culpable dissembling; the act variously judged a sin, a fault, or an excusable (if unwise) stratagem; whatever the judgment, the act sprang from the fear of man and the weakness of faith, and endangered Sarai and the promise, requiring God's intervention (v.17).

debates.question: Was Abram's calling Sarai 'my sister' a sin, or an excusable half-truth?

sides.position: A sin/fault of unbelief and the fear of man — a deception endangering Sarai's honor, springing from weak faith
An equivocation literally true (she was his half-sister, 20:12) — variously judged a culpable dissembling or an excusable stratagem of self-preservation

themes: Say, I pray thee, thou art my sister: that it may be well with me for thy sake; and my soul shall live because of thee
Abram's request that Sarai call herself his sister (the crux of the deception)
a sin of unbelief and the fear of man vs an equivocation literally true (his half-sister, 20:12)
the weakness of faith in the father of the faithful, endangering the promise

schools.summary: The Egyptians behold Sarai: 'and it came to pass, that, when Abram was come into Egypt, the Egyptians beheld the woman that she was very fair' — on entering Egypt, the Egyptians seeing and remarking Sarai's great beauty (the very thing Abram feared, v.11, coming to pass, Sarai's beauty drawing the attention of the Egyptians, and the deception, v.13, set in motion); the Egyptians taking notice of Sarai (her beauty, remarkable even in her age, observed, the attention that would bring her to Pharaoh's notice, v.15, the danger Abram had anticipated); 'very fair' (Sarai's beauty exceptional, occasioning the trouble that followed, the deception, meant to protect Abram, exposing Sarai to being taken, the consequence of beauty and of the half-truth, the trial unfolding as Abram feared, yet under God's overruling providence) — Gill, Henry.

themes: And it came to pass, that, when Abram was come into Egypt, the Egyptians beheld the woman that she was very fair
the Egyptians beholding Sarai's beauty
the fear of Abram fulfilled, the danger unfolding
Sarai's beauty occasioning the trouble that followed

schools.summary: Sarai taken into Pharaoh's house: 'the princes also of Pharaoh saw her, and commended her before Pharaoh: and the woman was taken into Pharaoh's house' — Pharaoh's courtiers (princes, officials) seeing Sarai and praising her beauty to the king (the attention rising from the people to the court, Sarai commended to Pharaoh as a woman fit for his harem, the danger coming to its height); 'the woman was taken into Pharaoh's house' (Sarai taken into Pharaoh's house, his harem, with a view to becoming his wife, the deception, v.13, meant to protect Abram, now exposing Sarai to being taken by the king, the promise itself, the seed through Sarai, endangered, the patriarch's stratagem having brought his wife into peril); the taking of Sarai the grave consequence of the deception (yet God would intervene to protect her honor and the covenant promise, v.17, the providence of God overruling the danger that Abram's weak faith had brought, the LORD watching over Sarai and the promised seed) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Clarke.

themes: The princes also of Pharaoh saw her, and commended her before Pharaoh: and the woman was taken into Pharaoh's house
Sarai's being commended to Pharaoh and taken into his house
the deception exposing Sarai to the king and endangering the promise
awaiting God's intervention to protect her and the covenant

schools.summary: Abram enriched by Pharaoh: 'and he entreated Abram well for her sake: and he had sheep, and oxen, and he asses, and menservants, and maidservants, and she asses, and camels' — Pharaoh treating Abram well for Sarai's sake (supposing him her brother), enriching him with flocks, herds, servants, and camels (Abram gaining great wealth through the deception, the gifts of the king, given on a false supposition); Pharaoh, taking Sarai as his intended wife, honoring her supposed brother Abram with gifts (the favor shown to Abram the fruit of the deception, the patriarch enriched, but at the cost of his wife's peril and his own integrity); the wealth Abram gained — flocks, herds, servants, camels — coming through the very deception that endangered Sarai and the promise (the gifts a doubtful gain, won by a fault, the riches of the patriarch, increased here, later the occasion of the strife with Lot, 13:6-7, the providence of God permitting Abram to prosper even through his fault, yet the gain tainted) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Clarke.

themes: And he entreated Abram well for her sake: and he had sheep, and oxen, and he asses, and menservants, and maidservants, and she asses, and camels
Abram's enrichment by Pharaoh for Sarai's sake
the wealth gained through the deception, a doubtful gain
the riches later the occasion of the strife with Lot (13:6-7)

schools.summary: The LORD plagues Pharaoh: 'and the LORD plagued Pharaoh and his house with great plagues because of Sarai Abram's wife' — God intervening, sending plagues (afflictions, perhaps diseases) upon Pharaoh and his household (the divine protection of Sarai and the covenant promise, God, not Abram's wit, preserving Sarai's honor and the line of the promised seed, the LORD watching over his own); the plagues coming 'because of Sarai Abram's wife' (God plaguing Pharaoh on account of Sarai, to prevent her being defiled and to deliver her, God's regard to the covenant and to Sarai, the mother of the promised seed, the plagues forcing Pharaoh to discover the truth and to restore Sarai to Abram, vv.18-19, God's faithful protection of the promise, despite Abram's fault); God's intervention showing that the safety of Sarai and the promise rested not on Abram's stratagem but on God's faithfulness (the LORD overruling the danger that Abram's weak faith had brought, the deliverance of Sarai God's work, the providence of God preserving the covenant line) — Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And the LORD plagued Pharaoh and his house with great plagues because of Sarai Abram's wife
the LORD's plaguing of Pharaoh to protect Sarai
God preserving Sarai's honor and the covenant line
the deliverance resting on God's faithfulness, not Abram's stratagem

schools.summary: Pharaoh rebukes Abram: 'and Pharaoh called Abram, and said, What is this that thou hast done unto me? why didst thou not tell me that she was thy wife?' — Pharaoh, learning the truth (by the plagues, v.17), summoning and rebuking Abram (the heathen king reproving the patriarch for the deception that had brought trouble on his house, the humbling of Abram, rebuked by a pagan for his fault); Pharaoh reproaching Abram — 'why didst thou not tell me that she was thy wife?' — for concealing the truth and exposing him to the danger of taking another man's wife (the justice of the rebuke, the patriarch, who should have been a witness of the truth, reproved by a heathen for his deception, the shame of Abram's fault); the rebuke of the believer by an unbeliever a humbling thing (Abram's deception, meant to save himself, dishonoring his faith and his God before the heathen, the fault of God's servant bringing reproach, yet God's faithfulness delivering him, and the rebuke deserved, a warning against the fear of man and the resort to deception) — Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And Pharaoh called Abram, and said, What is this that thou hast done unto me? why didst thou not tell me that she was thy wife?
Pharaoh's rebuke of Abram for the deception
the heathen king justly reproving the patriarch's fault
the humbling of the believer, the shame of Abram's deception

schools.summary: Pharaoh restores Sarai: 'why saidst thou, She is my sister? so I might have taken her to me to wife: now therefore behold thy wife, take her, and go thy way' — Pharaoh reproaching Abram for the half-truth that had nearly led him to take Sarai as his wife (the deception having brought Pharaoh to the brink of a great wrong, unwittingly taking another's wife, the king's just complaint at the danger Abram's words had caused); Pharaoh restoring Sarai to Abram — 'behold thy wife, take her, and go thy way' (the king, having learned the truth and feeling the plagues, v.17, returning Sarai untouched and bidding Abram depart, the deliverance of Sarai, by God's overruling providence, the promise preserved, Sarai restored to her husband); Pharaoh's restoring of Sarai and his dismissal of Abram showing God's hand in delivering Sarai and the covenant (the very king who took her now gladly sending her away, God's faithfulness, working even through the heathen king, preserving the promise, Abram, rebuked but delivered, departing with his wife) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin.

themes: Why saidst thou, She is my sister?... now therefore behold thy wife, take her, and go thy way
Pharaoh restoring Sarai and dismissing Abram
Sarai restored untouched by God's overruling providence
the covenant line preserved, Abram rebuked but delivered

schools.summary: Abram sent away from Egypt: 'and Pharaoh commanded his men concerning him: and they sent him away, and his wife, and all that he had' — Pharaoh giving his men orders concerning Abram, to conduct him safely out of Egypt (the king, anxious to be rid of the cause of the plagues, arranging for Abram's departure, the patriarch escorted out, with his wife and goods, under Pharaoh's protection); 'they sent him away, and his wife, and all that he had' (Abram sent away with Sarai restored and all his possessions, including the wealth gained, v.16, the patriarch departing unharmed, with his household and goods, God's providence bringing him safely out of Egypt, the promise preserved, despite his fault); Abram leaving Egypt richer in goods but humbled by his fault (the deliverance from Egypt, by God's mercy, restoring him to the land of promise, 13:1, the providence of God, faithful through Abram's weakness, bringing him out with his wife and substance, the patriarch returning to Canaan, to renew his pilgrimage and worship) — Gill, Clarke, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And Pharaoh commanded his men concerning him: and they sent him away, and his wife, and all that he had
Abram's being sent away from Egypt with his household
the patriarch brought safely out of Egypt by God's providence
the promise preserved despite his fault, to return to the land of promise
